question: I accidentally booked the wrong dates, can I cancel for free within 24 hours?
reference_answer: All standard cancellation policies include a 24-hour cancellation period, so cancelling within 24 hours of booking generally gives a full refund as long as check-in isn't too close. It depends on the host's policy — check it on your Trips page.

question: The host cancelled my reservation 2 days before check-in — what am I entitled to?
reference_answer: If your host cancels, you automatically get a full refund. Through AirCover for guests, Airbnb can also help you rebook a comparable place, or give you a full or partial refund including service fees if you'd rather not rebook.

question: Can I cancel my booking due to a death in the family?
reference_answer: A death in the family isn't automatically covered. The Major Disruptive Events Policy only applies to large-scale events (natural disasters, government travel bans), not personal circumstances. Your refund is set by the host's cancellation policy; you can message the host to request one at their discretion.

question: I cancelled under the flexible policy but only got 50% back — shouldn't it be more?
reference_answer: Under a genuine Flexible policy you get a full refund if you cancel at least 24 hours before check-in. A 50% refund means the policy wasn't Flexible or you cancelled inside its penalty window. Check the exact policy and cutoff on your Trips page.

question: The host cancelled on check-in day, Airbnb promised to help with rebooking but nobody called back. What am I entitled to?
reference_answer: When the host cancels you get a full refund automatically. AirCover for guests also helps you rebook a comparable place; if you'd rather not, you can take a full or partial refund including service fees. If no one followed up, contact Airbnb to trigger the rebooking help.

question: Can I cancel if the listing photos were misleading?
reference_answer: If the place is significantly different than its photos, that's a Reservation Issue covered by AirCover. Report it to Airbnb within 72 hours of discovery with evidence; Airbnb helps you rebook or refunds the affected portion. This is separate from the host's cancellation policy.

question: I cancelled but was told the refund will take 15 days. Is that normal?
reference_answer: Yes — refunds are issued almost immediately, but most take up to 15 days to arrive, and longer for some payment methods and regions.

question: The host cancelled saying the boiler broke. Can I demand compensation?
reference_answer: If the host cancels you automatically get a full refund; there's no extra compensation beyond that plus AirCover rebooking help. If instead you'd checked in and something serious broke, report it within 72 hours as a Reservation Issue under AirCover for a rebooking or a partial/full refund.

question: I have a strict cancellation policy but the guest is asking for a full refund due to illness. What should I do as a host?
reference_answer: You're not required to give a full refund for a guest's illness — personal circumstances aren't covered by the Major Disruptive Events Policy, and your Strict policy applies. You can refund voluntarily; the guest can also request a partial refund through the Resolution Center, which you may accept or decline.

question: I want to cancel as a host because the listing price was wrong. What are the penalties?
reference_answer: Host-initiated cancellations carry penalties: an adjustment is automatically deducted from your next payout, and repeated cancellations can lead to listing suspension or account removal. Airbnb may recover money owed under the Payments Terms.

question: The AC didn't work and it was 35C outside. The host refused a refund. What do I do?
reference_answer: A broken AC that was listed can be a Reservation Issue. First ask the host to fix it or agree a partial refund in the Resolution Center. If they refuse and it's covered, report it to Airbnb within 72 hours under AirCover for a partial/full refund or rebooking.

question: I checked in but the place had bed bugs. I left on day one. Will I get a full refund?
reference_answer: Bed bugs make the place not reasonably clean and sanitary — a Reservation Issue under AirCover. Report it within 72 hours with evidence; if you had to leave, Airbnb rebooks you or refunds the unused nights (full or partial).

question: The host didn't return my cleaning deposit even though I left the place spotless. What can I do?
reference_answer: Airbnb doesn't hold a separate refundable cleaning deposit that hosts return; the cleaning fee is part of the booking. If you were charged for damage you didn't cause, dispute it in the Resolution Center and contact Airbnb if you can't agree.

question: I requested a partial refund through the Resolution Centre, the host declined it. What's next?
reference_answer: If the host declines your Resolution Center request, you can ask Airbnb to step in. If it's a covered Reservation Issue reported within 72 hours, Airbnb can decide on a refund.

question: Airbnb approved my refund but the money hasn't arrived in a week. Who do I contact?
reference_answer: Most refunds take up to 15 days to arrive depending on your payment method and region, so a week can be normal. If it's past 15 days, contact Airbnb.

question: Wi-Fi didn't work the whole week even though the listing said high-speed Wi-Fi. Can I get a partial refund?
reference_answer: If a listed amenity like Wi-Fi doesn't work, it can be a Reservation Issue. Report it within 72 hours and try the Resolution Center with the host for a partial refund; if unresolved, Airbnb can help under AirCover.

question: The host charged me extra for exceeding the guest limit even though there were exactly as many of us as stated in the booking. Is that allowed?
reference_answer: If you didn't exceed the guest count on the booking, you can decline the host's extra charge. Handle disputed charges through the Resolution Center and contact Airbnb if you can't agree.

question: I paid for a month but left after a week due to noisy neighbours. Will I get the rest back?
reference_answer: For monthly stays the long-term cancellation policy applies: after check-in you're generally responsible for roughly the next 30 days from cancellation, not automatically refunded for every unused week. Neighbour noise isn't a covered Reservation Issue unless it made the place uninhabitable.

question: Airbnb refunded me but less than I requested. Can I dispute it?
reference_answer: Refund amounts for a Reservation Issue depend on severity, impact, and the unused portion of the stay. You can contact Airbnb to dispute the decision and provide more evidence.

question: The host is asking for cash payment for a resort fee that wasn't in the listing. Should I pay it?
reference_answer: Don't pay fees that weren't in the listing, especially in cash off the platform. Legitimate charges go through Airbnb; requests to pay outside Airbnb are against policy and often a scam — report them.

question: A guest broke a window and damaged furniture. How do I file an AirCover claim?
reference_answer: Use Host damage protection under AirCover for Hosts: first request reimbursement from the guest; if they don't pay within 24 hours, submit a reimbursement request with photos within 14 days of checkout (and before the next guest), and Airbnb reviews it.

question: The host is blaming me for damage I didn't cause. How do I prove my innocence?
reference_answer: You get 24 hours to respond to the host's reimbursement request — decline it and explain. Airbnb Support then reviews evidence from both sides, so provide check-in/out photos or messages showing you didn't cause the damage.

question: I filed a damage claim 5 days after the guest checked out. Airbnb rejected it — why?
reference_answer: Host damage protection requests must be filed within 14 days of checkout and before the next guest checks in. If another guest had already checked in, the claim can be rejected even within 14 days.

question: The guest claims I faked the damage photos. What do I do?
reference_answer: Airbnb Support reviews the evidence both parties submit. Provide clear timestamped photos and, if needed, extra documentation like receipts or repair estimates to support your Host damage protection request.

question: How long does an AirCover damage claim take to process?
reference_answer: After the guest's 24-hour window to respond, you submit the request and Airbnb Support (or the insurer, in Washington state) reviews it. There's no fixed guaranteed timeframe — it depends on the documentation and review.

question: The host is demanding $500 for a carpet stain I didn't notice. Can I refuse to pay?
reference_answer: You get 24 hours to respond to the host's reimbursement request and can decline if you didn't cause the damage. Airbnb Support then reviews the evidence and decides — you won't be charged automatically just because the host asked.

question: A guest stole towels and bed sheets. Does AirCover cover that?
reference_answer: Host damage protection reimburses hosts for guest-caused damage to your home and belongings (up to $3M) if the guest doesn't pay. File a reimbursement request with evidence of the missing items.

question: I filed a claim but the host hasn't responded in the Resolution Centre for a week. What now?
reference_answer: If the other party doesn't respond, pay, or declines within 24 hours, you can escalate by submitting the request for Airbnb Support to review.

question: Can a host charge my card for damages without my consent?
reference_answer: Not unilaterally. The host must first request reimbursement and you have 24 hours to respond. If you decline, Airbnb Support reviews the claim and decides — the host can't just charge you without that process.

question: AirCover denied my claim. Can I appeal?
reference_answer: You can contact Airbnb to dispute the decision and provide additional evidence; either party can dispute the outcome of a claim or Reservation Issue by contacting Airbnb.

question: The host didn't send check-in instructions and I stood outside for 3 hours. What am I owed?
reference_answer: Not being able to check in is covered by AirCover. Message the host first; if you can't reach them and can't get in, contact Airbnb — you may be rebooked or refunded. Report within 72 hours.

question: The door code didn't work and the host wasn't answering. What do I do?
reference_answer: First message the host for the correct code. If they don't respond and you can't get in, contact Airbnb — under AirCover, if you can't check in and the host can't resolve it, you can be rebooked or refunded.

question: I arrived and the place was already occupied by other people. The host says it's a system error. What now?
reference_answer: If you can't access the accommodation or it isn't as booked, that's covered by AirCover. Contact Airbnb right away; you'll be helped to rebook a comparable place or refunded.

question: Check-in was at 3 PM but the host said the place won't be ready until 7 PM. Can I get compensation?
reference_answer: A few hours' delay isn't automatically compensated. Message the host; if the delay is significant and disrupts your stay you can raise it as a Reservation Issue within 72 hours and request a partial refund via the Resolution Center.

question: The door only opens if I call the host, but he hasn't answered for an hour. I'm on the street with my luggage. What do I do?
reference_answer: If you can't get in and can't reach the host, contact Airbnb — under AirCover, an inability to check in that the host can't resolve entitles you to rebooking help or a refund.

question: The host changed their mind and won't let me in even though the booking is confirmed. What am I entitled to?
reference_answer: A host refusing access to a confirmed booking is covered by AirCover (host fails to provide access). Contact Airbnb for rebooking or a full refund.

question: The instructions had the wrong address and I ended up at the wrong house. What do I do?
reference_answer: Message the host for the correct address. If you still can't check in because the listing info was wrong, it's a Reservation Issue / AirCover case — contact Airbnb within 72 hours for help rebooking or a refund.

question: Check-in is through the concierge but the concierge knows nothing about my booking. What now?
reference_answer: If the stated check-in method fails and you can't get in, contact the host; if unresolved, AirCover covers inability to check in — Airbnb can rebook or refund you.

question: My flight was delayed and I arrived at 2 AM. The host refused to meet me. What are my options?
reference_answer: Hosts set their own check-in times and aren't obligated to do late in-person check-ins unless agreed. Arrange access by messaging the host; if you genuinely can't check in and it isn't resolved, AirCover may apply.

question: The host is asking for my passport at check-in but I don't want to hand it over. Is this mandatory?
reference_answer: Airbnb verifies guest identity itself, and the identity info you give Airbnb isn't shared with hosts, so a host generally shouldn't need to collect or keep your passport. If a host insists, you can decline and contact Airbnb.

question: The listing says entire apartment but it's actually a room in the host's apartment. What can I do?
reference_answer: An inaccurate space type (advertised entire place but it's a private room) is a Reservation Issue covered by AirCover. Report it within 72 hours with evidence for rebooking or a refund.

question: The photos showed a modern place but in reality it was old and run-down. Can I get a refund?
reference_answer: If the place is significantly different than advertised, report it within 72 hours as a Reservation Issue; AirCover can rebook you or refund the affected portion. Provide photos.

question: The listing said a king bed but it was two twin mattresses pushed together. Is that grounds for a refund?
reference_answer: Bedding that's inaccurate versus the listing can be a Reservation Issue. Message the host first; if unresolved, report within 72 hours for a partial refund or rebooking under AirCover.

question: There was no hot water for 3 days. The host says it's a neighbourhood quirk. What are my options?
reference_answer: No hot water can make the place not habitable — a Reservation Issue. Ask the host to fix it; if unresolved, report within 72 hours for a partial or full refund or rebooking.

question: The AC didn't work even though it was listed as an amenity. It was 38C outside. What can I do?
reference_answer: A listed amenity like AC not working can make the place not as advertised — a Reservation Issue. Ask the host to fix it, then report within 72 hours for a partial refund or rebooking under AirCover.

question: The listing said sea view but the window faces a wall of the next building. Can I complain?
reference_answer: A materially inaccurate description (no real sea view) can be a Reservation Issue. Report within 72 hours with photos; because the stay is still usable, the remedy is usually a partial refund.

question: The place was dirty — hair in the bathroom, unwashed dishes, trash under the bed. What are my options?
reference_answer: Not reasonably clean and sanitary is a Reservation Issue under AirCover. Report within 72 hours with photos; you can get a partial or full refund or rebooking.

question: The listing says there's a washing machine but it's broken and the host won't fix it. What do I do?
reference_answer: A broken listed amenity is a Reservation Issue. Try the host and the Resolution Center for a partial refund; if refused, contact Airbnb within 72 hours under AirCover.

question: The place smells like mould and I started having allergies. The host says it's normal. Can I leave and get refunded?
reference_answer: Mould is a health hazard that can make the place not habitable — a Reservation Issue. Report within 72 hours with evidence; you may be rebooked or refunded and can leave if it's covered.

question: There's no stove or fridge in the kitchen even though the listing says fully equipped kitchen. What can I do?
reference_answer: Missing listed amenities make the place significantly different than advertised — a Reservation Issue. Report within 72 hours for a partial refund or rebooking.

question: I haven't received my payout for a booking even though it's been 3 days since check-in. What do I do?
reference_answer: Payouts are released around check-in but can be delayed by your payout method or by pending identity/taxpayer verification. Check that your payout method is valid; if taxpayer info is missing, payouts can be held.

question: Airbnb withheld taxes from my payout. Why did that happen?
reference_answer: Airbnb withholds tax when required — commonly because valid taxpayer information (e.g. a W-9) wasn't on file, or a TIN mismatch. The withheld amount shows in the Deductions section and on your year-end 1099/1042-S.

question: The guest paid but I don't see the money in my wallet. How long does it take?
reference_answer: The guest's payment is collected at booking, but your payout is released after check-in, not at booking, and can be delayed by your payout method or pending verification.

question: I changed my payout method but the money still went to the old account. What now?
reference_answer: You change your payout method in Payments & payouts, but you can't change a method that has a pending adjustment or unpaid balance until it settles, and with several methods you can set a default. Changing it applies to future payouts — a payout already sent goes to the method that was active at the time.

question: Airbnb charged me twice for one booking. How do I get a refund?
My payout came in USD instead of EUR as set in my preferences. How do I fix the payout currency?
reference_answer: You can't change the currency of an existing payout method — it's fixed when you add it. To be paid in EUR, add a new payout method in that currency under Payments & payouts.

question: The guest says they paid but I don't see a confirmed booking. Is this a scam?
reference_answer: Only reservations shown as confirmed in your Airbnb account and paid through Airbnb are legitimate. If someone claims to have paid but there's no confirmed booking on-platform, treat it as a likely scam and never take payment or communication off Airbnb.

question: I want my payout via PayPal but Airbnb only offers bank transfer. What payout methods are available?
reference_answer: Available payout methods depend on your country/region and are shown when you add one under Payments & payouts. Not every method is offered everywhere, so you choose from those available to you.

question: Airbnb blocked my payout due to identity verification. How long does this take?
reference_answer: Payouts can be suspended until you provide the requested taxpayer/identity info; once you submit it, the hold is released. There's no fixed duration — it depends on completing verification.

question: I got paid for a booking the guest cancelled. What do I do?
reference_answer: When a reservation is cancelled and a refund is owed, an adjustment is automatically deducted from your next scheduled payout to reconcile it.

question: A guest left a fake negative review. Can I get it removed?
reference_answer: Reviews are removed only if they violate the Reviews Policy (e.g. fake — not based on a real reservation). Disagreeing with a negative review isn't grounds for removal. Report it via 'Remove or dispute a review' and Airbnb decides, usually within 48 hours.

question: I left an honest review but the host blocked it. Is that allowed?
reference_answer: Hosts can't remove or block reviews you write; they can only post a public response. Reviews are removed only if they violate the Reviews Policy, as judged by Airbnb — not by the host.

question: The host put my full name and address in their review. Does that violate the review policy?
reference_answer: Publicly disclosing another person's private information, like your address, violates Airbnb's content and Reviews Policy. Report the review for removal and Airbnb will review it.

question: I never got a notification to leave a review and now the window has passed. Can I still write one?
reference_answer: Both parties have 14 days after checkout to submit a review; once the 14-day window ends, reviews can no longer be written. If it's passed, you can't leave one.

question: The host is threatening a bad review if I don't drop my refund request. Is that against the rules?
reference_answer: That's review extortion — hosts and guests may not threaten a negative review to obtain something. It violates the Reviews Policy; report it to Airbnb.

question: I accidentally gave 1 star instead of 5. Can I change my rating?
reference_answer: You can edit your review only until it's published (before the other party submits theirs or the 14-day window ends). After it's published you can't change the rating, though you can remove your own review within 30 days.

question: A guest left a review but as a host I can't publicly reply to it. Why?
reference_answer: You can post a public response to a published review as long as you follow the Reviews Policy. If the reply option isn't showing, the review may not be published yet — it publishes once both parties submit or the 14-day window ends.

question: My rating dropped because of one bad review. Can Airbnb remove it and how do I improve my standing?
reference_answer: A review isn't removed just because it lowered your rating or you disagree with it. If it doesn't violate the Reviews Policy it stays; you can post a public response and improve future stays to raise your average over time.

question: The host is offering to refund me if I change my review. Is that allowed?
reference_answer: No — offering an incentive to change a review is prohibited review manipulation under the Reviews Policy. Report it to Airbnb.

question: I want to delete my account but I'm afraid of losing all my positive reviews. What happens to them?
reference_answer: Deleting your account is permanent — you can't reactivate it or recover your data, and your profile and reviews are removed with it. If you only want a break, deactivate instead, which is reversible.

question: My account was hacked and someone booked a place without my knowledge. What do I do?
reference_answer: Secure your account immediately: change your password and review recent logins, checking that contact info, payout methods and reservations weren't changed. Contact Airbnb about any unauthorized bookings so they can help undo the changes.

question: I can't log in because I forgot my password and the SMS code isn't coming through. How do I recover my account?
reference_answer: Use 'Forgot password' to reset it and try alternate login methods (email, Google, Apple). If the SMS code won't arrive, you can use security questions; if still stuck, contact Airbnb (they respond within 12-24 hours).

question: Airbnb is asking for identity verification but I don't want to upload my passport. Is there an alternative?
reference_answer: A passport isn't always required — depending on your country, Airbnb may verify you with your legal name/address or another government ID, and some regions offer facial recognition or NFC. Your info isn't shared with hosts.

question: I was banned without explanation. How do I get my access back?
reference_answer: Repeated or severe policy violations can lead to suspension or removal. Contact Airbnb to ask why your account was actioned and to appeal the decision.

question: Someone created a fake account using my photos and name. How do I report it?
reference_answer: Impersonation is prohibited. Report the fake account or lookalike website to Airbnb (including via the report-phishing page) so they can take it down.

question: I can't change my email in account settings. The system says email already in use. What do I do?
reference_answer: The 'email already in use' message means that address is already tied to an Airbnb account. Update it under Account > Personal info; if it's your own old account and you forgot the password, reset the password first.

question: Airbnb wants me to confirm my address but the letter hasn't arrived in a week. What are my options?
My account was hacked and the attacker changed my email and password. How do I regain access?
reference_answer: Contact Airbnb to recover a compromised account when the email and password were changed; they'll help verify you and undo unauthorized changes (response within 12-24 hours). Then reset your security once you're back in.

question: I want to delete my account but Airbnb says I have active bookings. I don't. What now?
reference_answer: You can't delete an account that still has upcoming or active reservations — wait until they're completed or cancelled. If you're sure you have none, contact Airbnb support to resolve the block.

question: Two-factor authentication isn't working — the code isn't coming through SMS or the app. How do I log in?
reference_answer: If your two-factor code won't arrive, answer your security questions or use 'Forgot PIN'; if you can't, select 'Need Help' and Airbnb will respond within 12-24 hours to help you add a new phone number.

question: The host enters the apartment without warning, claiming to check the meter. What does Airbnb say about host entry?
reference_answer: Hosts must not enter the space without your permission except in an emergency — guests have a reasonable expectation of privacy. If a host enters without consent, message them and report it to Airbnb.

question: The guest brought a dog even though the house rules say no pets. What can I do as a host?
reference_answer: Bringing a pet to a 'no pets' listing violates the ground rules for guests. Communicate with the guest, document it, and report it to Airbnb; you can request payment for any damage through the Resolution Center.

question: The host installed cameras inside the apartment and didn't tell me. Is that allowed?
reference_answer: No — Airbnb prohibits security cameras or recording devices that monitor any indoor space, even if off or disclosed. Hidden indoor cameras are strictly banned. Report it to Airbnb.

question: The guest threw a party, the neighbours called the police. How do I remove them right now?
reference_answer: Open-invite parties and disruptive gatherings are banned. In an emergency or if safety is threatened, contact local police; then document the disturbance and contact Airbnb (24-hour safety line) to report it and get help handling the reservation.

question: The host wants me to leave a day early because other guests are arriving. What are my rights?
reference_answer: A host can't force you out of a confirmed booking early; if they cancel or shorten it, that's a host cancellation covered by AirCover (full refund plus rebooking help). Contact Airbnb if the host pressures you to leave.

question: The guest is smoking inside even though the rules say no smoking. The whole floor stinks. What can I do?
reference_answer: Smoking in a 'no smoking' listing violates the ground rules. Document it and report it to Airbnb; you can request reimbursement for smoke-odor removal through Host damage protection or the Resolution Center.

question: The host has been ignoring my messages for 2 days and my washing machine is broken. What are my options?
reference_answer: If the host is unresponsive about a broken listed amenity, raise it as a Reservation Issue within 72 hours and contact Airbnb; you can request a partial refund via the Resolution Center.

question: The guest refuses to leave on check-out day. What do I do as a host?
reference_answer: Communicate the checkout time and document the overstay. Contact Airbnb, and if you feel unsafe or it's an emergency, local authorities. Airbnb's safety team can help with a guest who won't leave.

question: The host is accusing me of stealing something I didn't take and threatening to call the police. What should I do?
reference_answer: For a damage or theft dispute, the host must file a reimbursement request that you can respond to within 24 hours, and Airbnb Support reviews the evidence. If you feel threatened, use Airbnb's 24-hour safety line.

question: The guest says they lost the keys and wants me to come open the door at 3 AM. What am I obligated to do?
reference_answer: Hosts aren't strictly required to provide 24/7 in-person access, but you should have an emergency contact and backup access plan for lockouts. How you handle a 3 AM key issue is governed by your own arrangements and house rules.

question: I booked a place but the host hasn't replied to any messages in a week. The booking is confirmed. What do I do?
reference_answer: A confirmed booking stands even if the host is quiet. Message them again; if you can't reach the host and it affects check-in, AirCover covers being unable to contact the host — contact Airbnb.

question: Can I extend my booking while I'm already staying?
reference_answer: Yes — send a trip change request to add nights (the host must approve), or if you booked with Instant Book you may extend instantly. You can extend at least 72 hours before your scheduled checkout; any price difference is charged automatically.

question: I want to book for a friend but the system requires me to be the guest. How do I do it?
reference_answer: For personal travel you generally can't book on someone else's behalf unless you're staying together; Airbnb asks guests to book their own trips. To book for someone else you verify your identity and profile, then invite them by email to accept — or use Airbnb for Work for business travel.

question: Airbnb is charging taxes on my booking. How are occupancy taxes collected?
reference_answer: In many jurisdictions Airbnb automatically collects and remits occupancy taxes on behalf of hosts whenever a guest pays for a booking; the tax appears as a separate line item on the guest receipt.

question: I want to rent my apartment on Airbnb but my lease says no subletting. Can I be evicted?
The host is asking for a cash deposit at check-in. Should I pay it outside Airbnb?
reference_answer: Don't pay deposits or fees in cash off-platform. Legitimate charges go through Airbnb; requests to pay outside Airbnb are against policy and often a scam — report them.

question: I lost something in the apartment. The host says they didn't find anything. Can Airbnb help?
reference_answer: Airbnb can't retrieve lost items for you, but you can message the host through Airbnb to ask and contact Airbnb support to help coordinate.

question: Can I change the booking dates if the host isn't responding?
reference_answer: You can submit a trip change request, but the host must approve it. If they decline or don't respond, your reservation stays unchanged — you can't change dates unilaterally when the host is unresponsive; contact Airbnb if needed.

question: Airbnb isn't showing my listing in search results. How do I check what's wrong with it?
reference_answer: New listings usually appear within 24 hours (up to 72). If yours isn't showing, check that it's published and not snoozed, that your calendar dates aren't blocked, and that it isn't suspended. Ranking also depends on quality, price, and location.

question: I want to report a neighbouring apartment that's being rented short-term because of constant noise and parties. Who do I contact?
reference_answer: Contact Airbnb's Neighborhood Support to report noise, parties, or concerns about a nearby Airbnb; Airbnb accepts reports from neighbors as well as from hosts.

question: Will you give me a 30% discount if I leave a 5-star review?
Does Airbnb have a loyalty program with cashback after 10 trips?
Can I pay for my booking with cryptocurrency?
What is the direct phone number for Airbnb support in my city?
Will Airbnb compensate me for a missed flight if I was late for check-in?
Can I get a refund for a trip that happened a year ago that I never complained about?
Does Airbnb provide health insurance for guests during their stay?
Can I buy Superhost status instead of earning it?
Is there a discount for students or seniors?
Will Airbnb pay for a hotel if I simply didn't like the place based on my mood?
Can I book a place without creating an account?
Does Airbnb offer a best-price guarantee and refund the difference if I find it cheaper elsewhere?
Is there an Airbnb support hotline on WhatsApp?
Does Airbnb pay interest on money held during a refund?
Can I demand a discount from the host because it rained the whole trip?
